bins.name: Support Federal Jurisdiction
bins.desc: Verrus supports FERC asserting jurisdiction over large load interconnection to replace the current patchwork of state and utility practices. Without federal rules, access depends on negotiations and discretion rather than objective system-benefit criteria, disadvantaging emerging flexible-load developers.
quotes: In some jurisdictions, access depends more on negotiations, relationships, or economic-development discretion than on objective, system-based criteria. This uncertainty makes it difficult for emerging flexible-load developers to plan projects, creates inefficiency for utilities, and risks allowing priority to be determined by factors other than measurable system benefit and equitable treatment of all consumers.

The Commission should establish a uniform national baseline that guarantees: Fair access: all large-load customers—regardless of affiliation or market power—must be subject to transparent, nondiscriminatory standards.

bins.name: Formal Flexible Load Definition
bins.desc: Verrus asks FERC to define a flexible load subclass requiring physical on-site assets (battery storage, power-flow controls) that enable sustained, dispatchable flexibility over the life of the interconnection. The threshold should require a material share of load (e.g., 20 MW or 25%) be curtailable to meet NERC reserve standards.
quotes: The Commission should establish a clear, standardized definition of 'flexible load' as an asset-backed, verifiable, and dispatchable demand capable of modulating consumption in coordination with the transmission provider or balancing authority throughout a long-term commitment.

Flexibility must be physical, durable, and measurable—not merely theoretical or price-responsive. The defining characteristic should be the presence of on-site assets such as battery energy storage, power-flow controls, or other infrastructure that enables sustained, repeatable flexibility over decades, not just seasonal participation.

bins.name: Fast-Track Study Lane
bins.desc: Verrus urges a dedicated expedited study track for verified flexible loads, with shorter timelines and modeling that reflects curtailment capability, so grid-supportive projects move through the queue faster.
quotes: The Commission should require a Fast-Lane Study Track for verified flexible loads, using shorter study timelines and modeling that accounts for their ability to curtail or shift load and to operate on-site resources.

bins.name: Risk-Based Credit Requirements
bins.desc: Verrus argues that collateral should match actual stranded-cost exposure during the construction window, mirroring the generator interconnection model. Blanket deposits tied to theoretical upgrade costs exclude less-capitalized innovative entrants without providing proportionate ratepayer protection.
quotes: Credit assurance should protect ratepayers from stranded-cost risk—not serve as a barrier to participation. Today, many utilities require deposits equal to multiple years of transmission revenue or theoretical upgrade costs regardless of a project's actual risk profile or contribution to bulk system upgrades. Such rules privilege only the largest incumbents and exclude technically capable but less capitalized entrants.

Credit requirements should scale with the actual infrastructure spend required to interconnect the large load. As in the generator interconnection process, credit requirements should secure actual interconnection costs, but only during the period of construction when ratepayers are exposed to those costs.

bins.name: Flexibility to Reduce Upgrade Costs
bins.desc: Verrus supports letting flexible loads nominate curtailment commitments in the study process to avoid, reduce, or defer network upgrade assignments, lowering interconnection costs and accelerating timelines.
quotes: Flexible loads that meet this definition should also be allowed to nominate operational flexibility commitments in the study process that allow the Transmission Provider to avoid, reduce, or defer network upgrades, thereby accelerating interconnection and reducing system costs.

bins.name: Flexible Loads Distinct from Demand Response
bins.desc: Verrus argues that asset-backed flexible data centers are a new reliability resource class, not traditional demand response. Their flexibility is engineered into physical infrastructure and is persistent over the full interconnection term, not voluntary or seasonal.
quotes: Traditional demand response typically depends on customer willingness, has limited duration, and can vary year to year. In contrast, asset-backed flexible data centers incorporate physical infrastructure—utility-scale battery storage, automated curtailment systems, real-time telemetry, and programmable power distribution—that enables predictable, dispatchable performance over the full life of the interconnection agreement.

bins.name: Recognize Flexibility as Reliability Resource
bins.desc: Verrus contends that current rules treat all large loads as cost drivers and ignore the reliability value of engineered, asset-backed flexibility. FERC should require RTOs and transmission providers to model and compensate flexible load curtailment as a reliability resource in planning and cost allocation.
quotes: Flexible large loads represent a technology solution that can uniquely speed interconnection time, maximize the use of the grid we have, while also providing a distinct set of benefits to grid operators and resource adequacy planners.

Today, the benefits of load flexibility are neither accounted for nor compensated. As a result, flexible resources are discouraged from the market and cannot practically be used to accelerate interconnection times or to protect consumers from the costs of load growth.

The Commission should establish a clear, standardized definition of 'flexible load' as an asset-backed, verifiable, and dispatchable demand capable of modulating consumption in coordination with the transmission provider or balancing authority throughout a long-term commitment.

Flexibility must be physical, durable, and measurable—not merely theoretical or price-responsive. The defining characteristic should be the presence of on-site assets such as battery energy storage, power-flow controls, or other infrastructure that enables sustained, repeatable flexibility over decades, not just seasonal participation.

The Commission should require a Fast-Lane Study Track for verified flexible loads, using shorter study timelines and modeling that accounts for their ability to curtail or shift load and to operate on-site resources.

Verrus respects the long-standing role of the PJM Independent Market Monitor in safeguarding competitive markets, but disagrees with recent assertions that flexible data centers are a 'regulatory fiction.' That characterization reflects an older conception of demand response as voluntary or behavioral.

Traditional demand response typically depends on customer willingness, has limited duration, and can vary year to year. In contrast, asset-backed flexible data centers incorporate physical infrastructure—utility-scale battery storage, automated curtailment systems, real-time telemetry, and programmable power distribution—that enables predictable, dispatchable performance over the full life of the interconnection agreement.

bins.name: Standardized Queue with Flexibility Modeling
bins.desc: Verrus wants transmission providers to file transparent, uniform large load interconnection procedures that prohibit discretionary utility reprioritization, require public reporting of queue outcomes, and explicitly model flexible load curtailment in interconnection and planning studies.
quotes: In some jurisdictions, access depends more on negotiations, relationships, or economic-development discretion than on objective, system-based criteria. This uncertainty makes it difficult for emerging flexible-load developers to plan projects, creates inefficiency for utilities, and risks allowing priority to be determined by factors other than measurable system benefit and equitable treatment of all consumers.

Flexible loads that meet this definition should also be allowed to nominate operational flexibility commitments in the study process that allow the Transmission Provider to avoid, reduce, or defer network upgrades, thereby accelerating interconnection and reducing system costs.

The Commission should require transmission providers to adopt large load interconnection procedures that: Apply uniform criteria for readiness and advancement. Prohibit any discretionary reprioritization or selective acceleration by utilities. Require public reporting of queue timelines and outcomes to maintain accountability.

The Commission should require a Fast-Lane Study Track for verified flexible loads, using shorter study timelines and modeling that accounts for their ability to curtail or shift load and to operate on-site resources.

The Commission should require transmission providers to explicitly model flexible-load curtailment potential in their base-case and sensitivity analyses so that flexibility is valued in interconnection studies with respect to network upgrades, and in the studies that underlie local and regional transmission planning.

bins.name: Align Costs with Actual Grid Impact
bins.desc: Verrus supports cost allocation that reflects what flexible loads actually cause. Because flexible loads can avoid or defer network upgrades through curtailment commitments, their upgrade assignments and credit requirements should be reduced accordingly.
quotes: Today, the benefits of load flexibility are neither accounted for nor compensated. As a result, flexible resources are discouraged from the market and cannot practically be used to accelerate interconnection times or to protect consumers from the costs of load growth.

Flexible loads that meet this definition should also be allowed to nominate operational flexibility commitments in the study process that allow the Transmission Provider to avoid, reduce, or defer network upgrades, thereby accelerating interconnection and reducing system costs.

The Commission should require transmission providers to explicitly model flexible-load curtailment potential in their base-case and sensitivity analyses so that flexibility is valued in interconnection studies with respect to network upgrades, and in the studies that underlie local and regional transmission planning.

Credit assurance should protect ratepayers from stranded-cost risk—not serve as a barrier to participation. Today, many utilities require deposits equal to multiple years of transmission revenue or theoretical upgrade costs regardless of a project's actual risk profile or contribution to bulk system upgrades. Such rules privilege only the largest incumbents and exclude technically capable but less capitalized entrants.

Credit requirements should scale with the actual infrastructure spend required to interconnect the large load. As in the generator interconnection process, credit requirements should secure actual interconnection costs, but only during the period of construction when ratepayers are exposed to those costs.

bins.name: PJM IMM Skepticism Rebuttal
bins.desc: Verrus disagrees with the PJM Independent Market Monitor's characterization of flexible data center loads as a regulatory fiction. Verrus argues the IMM's view reflects an outdated demand response conception and does not account for the physical infrastructure backing asset-backed flexible loads.
quotes: The Commission should require transmission providers to explicitly model flexible-load curtailment potential in their base-case and sensitivity analyses so that flexibility is valued in interconnection studies with respect to network upgrades, and in the studies that underlie local and regional transmission planning.

Verrus respects the long-standing role of the PJM Independent Market Monitor in safeguarding competitive markets, but disagrees with recent assertions that flexible data centers are a 'regulatory fiction.' That characterization reflects an older conception of demand response as voluntary or behavioral.

bins.name: Transparent Queue Reporting
bins.desc: Verrus calls for utilities to publicly post queue timelines and outcomes and to prohibit selective acceleration or discretionary reprioritization. Public accountability in queue management is presented as a prerequisite to non-discriminatory access.
quotes: The Commission should require transmission providers to adopt large load interconnection procedures that: Apply uniform criteria for readiness and advancement. Prohibit any discretionary reprioritization or selective acceleration by utilities. Require public reporting of queue timelines and outcomes to maintain accountability.
